--- SOURCE 1: Techno-Economic Analysis - NREL ---
URL: https://www.nrel.gov/analysis/techno-economic

SUMMARY:
The levelized cost of energy calculator provides a simple way to calculate both utility-scale and distributed generation renewable energy

FULL CONTENT:
![NREL logo](/images/libraries/global/nrel.svg?sfvrsn=d16c1421_4)

# Techno-Economic Analysis

NREL conducts supply chain and life-cycle analysis of energy technologies to identify key R&D directions and competitiveness opportunities.

![Photo of a group talking in front of a large display of information.](/images/libraries/analysis-images/eads-hero-group-talk-45444.jpg?sfvrsn=8bad4258_2 "eads-hero-group-talk-45444")

## Levelized Cost of Energy Calculator

The [levelized cost of energy calculator](/analysis/tech-lcoe) provides a simple way to calculate both utility-scale and distributed generation
renewable energy technologies that compares the combination of capital costs, operations
and maintenance, performance, and fuel costs.

## Manufacturing Analysis

Our manufacturing analysis yields insights to support industry decisions about R&D
targets, investment strategies, and policy evaluation. For more information, see the
[Advanced Manufacturing Research site](/manufacturing/analysis).

Also, learn about our [solar for industrial process heat analysis](/analysis/solar-industrial-process-heat).

## PVWatts

The [PVWatts® Calculator](https://pvwatts.nrel.gov/) estimates the energy production of grid-connected photovoltaic energy systems throughout
the world. It allows homeowners, small building owners, installers, and manufacturers
to easily develop estimates of the performance of potential PV installations.

## REopt

The [REopt®](/reopt) techno-economic decision support platform identifies the optimal mix of renewable
energy, conventional generation, storage, and electrification technologies to meet
cost savings, resilience, emissions reductions, and energy performance goals at a
site or portfolio of sites.

## Solar Technology Cost Analysis

Our solar technology cost analysis examines the technology costs and supply chain
issues for different solar photovoltaic technologies. Learn more about this analysis
on the [Solar Research site](/solar/market-research-analysis/solar-cost-analysis).

## System Advisor Model

The [System Advisor Model](https://sam.nrel.gov/) is a free desktop application for techno-economic analysis of energy technologies.
It is used by project managers and engineers, policy analysts, technology developers,
and researchers to investigate questions about the technical, economic, and financial
feasibility of renewable energy projects.

### Energy Analysis Delivered to Your Inbox

Be the first to know about the latest news, publications, events, and data and tool launches from the NREL Energy Analysis team. [See an example](https://mailchi.mp/nrel/energy-analysis-at-nrel-december-389317) before you sign up.

Your personal data will only be used for as long as you are subscribed. For more information, review the  [NREL security and privacy policy](https://www.nrel.gov/security).

Share

Last Updated June 27, 2025

![Alliance for Sustainable Energy, LLC](/images/libraries/global/alliance-logo-black.svg?sfvrsn=8de460bc_1 "alliance-logo-black")

A TEA assesses the overall value of a technology, allowing analysts to objectively weigh benefits against costs. 3 ÅMØ’s åpprøåçh tø TËÅ føçüsës øñ çøst bëñçhmårkïñg. vs. Ïñ å çøst bëñçhmårkïñg åpprøåçh, thë çøsts øf å ñëw tëçhñøløgÿ årë çømpårëd hëåd-tø-hëåd ågåïñst thøsë øf åñ ëxïstïñg çømmërçïål tëçhñøløgÿ thåt ït wøüld çømpëtë wïth ïñ thë mårkëtplåçë. Thïs ïs døñë tø åssëss çømpëtïtïvëñëss. AMO’s approach to TEA focuses on cost benchmarking. In a cost benchmarking approach, the costs of a new technology are compared head-to-head against those of the existing commercial technology that it would compete with in the marketplace. This is done to assess competitiveness. 4 ÇØST ÅSSËSSMËÑT Før süççëssfül vs. çømmërçïålïzåtïøñ, å ñëw tëçhñøløgÿ müst bë çøst-çømpëtïtïvë. Thë gøål ïs çøst pårïtÿ (åt lëåst!) whëñ çømpårïñg tø å çømmërçïål bëñçhmårk, åftër åççøüñtïñg før tëçhñïçål përførmåñçë bëñëfïts. For successful commercialization, a new technology must be cost-competitive. The goal is cost parity (at least!) when comparing to a commercial benchmark, after accounting for technical performance benefits. 5 MÅÑÜFÅÇTÜRÏÑG ÇØSTS Çåpïtål Ëxpëñsës (ÇåpËx) Øpëråtïñg Ëxpëñsës (ØpËx) (øñë-tïmë) (rëçürrïñg) Manufacturing costs can be broken down into two main categories: Capital Expenses and Operating Expenses. 6 MÅÑÜFÅÇTÜRÏÑG ÇØSTS Çåpïtål Ëxpëñsës (ÇåpËx) (øñë-tïmë) Büïldïñgs & Çøñstrüçtïøñ Ëqüïpmëñt • Øñë-tïmë fåçïlïtÿ çøsts • Åmørtïzëd øvër åssët’s üsëfül lïfëtïmë tø rëlåtë ÇåpËx tø å spëçïfïç prødüçtïøñ vølümë. Capital expenses, or CapEx, are non-recurring costs such as equipment, buildings, and construction. In a TEA, these one-time facility costs are amortized over the assets’ useful lifetime to relate CapEx to a specific production volume. 7 åtërïåls Ëñërgÿ MÅÑÜFÅÇTÜRÏÑG ÇØSTS Øpëråtïñg Ëxpëñsës (ØpËx) • Rëçürrïñg çøsts thåt çåñ bë ëïthër fï ør vårïåblë • Fïxëd ØpËx (ë.g., låbør) dø ñøt çhåñgë wïth prødüçtïøñ øütpüt • Vårïåblë ØpËx (ë.g., måtërïåls) årë dïrëçtlÿ tïëd tø prødüçtïøñ vølümë. M Låbør xëd (rëçürrïñg) Operating expenses, or OpEx, are recurring costs such as materials, labor, and energy. These recurring costs can be either fixed or variable.  Fixed operating expenses, like labor, do not change with production output; while  Variable operating expenses, like materials, are directly tied to production volume. 8 åtërïåls Ëñërgÿ MÅÑÜFÅÇTÜRÏÑG ÇØSTS Çåpïtål Ëxpëñsës (ÇåpËx) Øpëråtïñg Ëxpëñsës (ØpËx) (øñë-tïmë) (rëçürrïñg) Büïldïñgs & Çøñstrüçtïøñ Ëqüïpmëñt M Låbør We’ll discuss specific methods for estimating both CapEx and OpEx costs later in this series. 9 ÇØST BËÑÇHMÅRKÏÑG Måñüfåçtürïñg Çøst Çømpårïsøñ Δ Çøst ÇåpËx ØpËx ØpËx ÇåpËx Ñëw Çømmërçïål *åssümïñg dëpløÿmëñt åt ïñdüstrïål Tëçhñøløgÿ* Bëñçhmårk sçålë (ëvëñ ïf çürrëñt TRL ïs løw) In the cost benchmarking approach, we evaluate the commercial viability of the new technology by comparing its manufacturing costs to those of a corresponding commercial benchmark. For all cost estimations, we assume that the new technology has been deployed at a full industrial scale– even if its current technology readiness level, or TRL, is low. 10 ÇØST BËÑÇHMÅRKÏÑG Måñüfåçtürïñg Çøst Çømpårïsøñ Δ Çøst ØpËx ØpËx Bëñçhmårkïñg shøüld bë døñë øñ å përførmåñçë-ëqüïvålëñçë båsïs, üsïñg åñ åpprøprïåtë füñçtïøñål ÇåpËx üñït tø dëfïñë thë ÇåpËx rëfërëñçë qüåñtïtÿ. The use of a functional unit is particularly important if the two technologies differ significantly in size, function, or final product use, since the new technology might not substitute 1 to 1 for the benchmark technology. 11 ËXÅMPLË Çøst Çømpårïsøñ: Çåtålÿsts før Ëthÿlëñë Måñüfåçtürïñg Ñëw Bëñçhmårk vs. Çåtålÿst Çåtålÿst For example, let’s say we are performing a cost analysis for a new, advanced catalyst that will be used in ethylene manufacturing. The new catalyst will serve as a drop-in replacement for an existing commercial catalyst - the benchmark technology. 12 ËXÅMPLË Çøst Çømpårïsøñ: Çåtålÿsts før Ëthÿlëñë Måñüfåçtürïñg Çåtålÿst Måñüfåçtürïñg Çøst ($) ØpËx ØpËx ÇåpËx ÇåpËx Ñëw Bëñçhmårk vs. Çåtålÿst Çåtålÿst We’ll compare these technologies by summing the OpEx and CapEx components. 13 ËXÅMPLË Çøst Çømpårïsøñ: Çåtålÿsts før Ëthÿlëñë Måñüfåçtürïñg ØpËx hïghër çøst për pøüñd Δ Çåtålÿst Måñüfåçtürïñg Çøst ($) ØpËx për pøüñd ÇåpËx ÇåpËx Ñëw Bëñçhmårk vs. Çåtålÿst Çåtålÿst Imagine that the new catalyst will cost 10% more to manufacture, pound-for-pound, than the commercial benchmark technology. The new technology results in a higher cost per pound. 14 ËXÅMPLË Çøst Çømpårïsøñ: Çåtålÿsts før Ëthÿlëñë Måñüfåçtürïñg løwër çøst për vølümë ëqüïvålëñt Çåtålÿst ØpËx ØpËx Δ Måñüfåçtürïñg Çøst ($) për vølümë ÇåpËx ÇåpËx ëqüïvålëñt Ñëw Bëñçhmårk vs. Çåtålÿst Çåtålÿst However, a key benefit is that it will last 20% longer than the benchmark catalyst. Since the new catalyst won’t need to be replaced as frequently, the facility will consume less of the new catalyst to produce an equivalent volume of ethylene. 15 ËXÅMPLË Çøst Çømpårïsøñ: Çåtålÿsts før Ëthÿlëñë Måñüfåçtürïñg Çåtålÿst Måñüfåçtürïñg Çøst ($) ØpËx ØpËx për vølümë ÇåpËx ÇåpËx Thë üsë øf å përførmåñçë-ëqüïvålëñt ëqüïvålëñt füñçtïøñål üñït ëñåblës “åpplës-tø-Ñëw Bëñçhmårk åpplës” çøst çømpårïsøñs bëtwëëñ Çåtålÿst Çåtålÿst tëçhñøløgïës. In this example, after adjusting for performance equivalence, we found that the new technology provided a cost savings over the benchmark technology. The use of a performance-equivalent functional unit enables “apples-to-apples” cost comparisons between technologies. 16 Thåñks før wåtçhïñg! Ïñ thïs mødülë, wë prøvïdëd åñ øvërvïëw øf tëçhñø-ëçøñømïç åñålÿsïs åñd ïñtrødüçëd thë çøst bëñçhmårkïñg åpprøåçh üsëd bÿ ÅMØ. Ïñ fütürë tütørïål mødülës, wë’ll dïsçüss: • Mëthøds før ëstïmåtïñg ÇåpËx åñd ØpËx, ïñçlüdïñg ëstïmåtïøñ tëçhñïqüës før ëårlÿ-stågë tëçhñøløgïës • Çøñsïdëråtïøñs før dëfïñïñg thë çømmërçïål bëñçhmårk åñd ëståblïshïñg å përførmåñçë-ëqüïvålëñt füñçtïøñål üñït In this module, we provided an overview of techno­ economic analysis and introduced the cost benchmarking approach used by AMO. In future tutorial modules, we’ll discuss: • Methods for estimating CapEx and OpEx, including estimation techniques for early-stage technologies • Considerations for defining the commercial benchmark and establishing a performance-equivalent functional unit We’ll be applying the TEA concepts introduced in this module when carrying out those analysis steps. 17

--- SOURCE 3: A guide to techno-economic analysis for early-stage founders ---
URL: https://www.zerocarbon.vc/post/a-guide-to-techno-economic-analysis-for-early-stage-founders

SUMMARY:
## Why do a TEA? A techno-economic analysis (TEA) is an evaluation tool that combines technical and economic aspects to assess the feasibility, competitiveness, and potential impact of a technology or project. You can use a TEA to drive your strategy and decision-making: by understanding the most important metrics influencing the unit economics and commercial viability, you can start to build a roadmap to bring down cost drivers or optimise important performance parameters. Given that many climate technologies depend heavily on electricity prices, and a significant number, on the cost of hydrogen, carbon dioxide or both, we thought it would be helpful to discuss the numbers here. What is a TEA?

# [What's new?](https://www.zerocarbon.vc/news)

# A guide to techno-economic analysis for early-stage founders

![Writer: Max Werny]()

Updated: Apr 22

![](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/39490a_fa0c7681e494437bb83edff3525d340d~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_49,h_28,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/39490a_fa0c7681e494437bb83edff3525d340d~mv2.png)

There’s nothing like a good TEA. And no, this (unfortunately) does not have anything to do with Earl Grey, Darjeeling or matcha tea. The TEA we’re talking about stands for **techno-economic analysis** and is all about **understanding your business** and building for success!

Given its importance for every hard tech startup, we decided to put together a short, concise TEA guide for early-stage founders. A TEA can seem daunting at first but very often turns out to be an incredibly useful tool for founders. Our mission is to make this journey slightly less bumpy and therefore more enjoyable for entrepreneurs. Read on to find out more!

## **Why do a TEA?**

A techno-economic analysis (TEA) is an evaluation tool that **combines technical and economic aspects to assess the feasibility, competitiveness, and potential impact of a technology or project**. It can provide you with a detailed understanding of your unit economics, cost drivers, technical key performance indicators (KPIs), as well as the benefits and risks associated with the implementation of your technology.

**You can use a TEA to drive your strategy and decision-making**: by understanding the most important metrics influencing the unit economics and commercial viability, you can start to build a roadmap to bring down cost drivers or optimise important performance parameters.

As every TEA is different, we recommend founders to use multiple resources to construct a TEA that fits their process or product (more on this at the end of the article). TEAs for pre-seed and seed companies will naturally differ from TEAs for later-stage companies as there are more unknown parameters and thus greater uncertainty. But don’t let this deter you - **a TEA is a tool that comes in handy even at the earliest stages of company building**!

Really, a TEA is living document that evolves with your company, and one that grows in fidelity as you get greater confidence in the input parameters. Most crucially, remember that it isn’t simply a tick-box exercise for investors to show you have one in your data room.

## **What goes into a TEA?**

Like a good cuppa tea, you need to get the ingredients right - see our graphic below for the most essential ingredients:

![](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/39490a_89ed8390f6364fdcb9645c5683dd5907~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_49,h_28,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/39490a_89ed8390f6364fdcb9645c5683dd5907~mv2.png)

*Relevant information that should go into a techno-economic analysis (list is not exhaustive).*

For those of you who want to get to the bottom of this, take a look at our TEA checklist below (list is not exhaustive):

A **schematic figure** (e.g. process flow diagram), especially if the system is complex

A list or table of the key components in your system

A concise technological description of the system, process or product that you are working on (if this is not already present in your data room)

**Key technical parameters**:

Mass balance (mass flows in and out of your system)

Energy balance (electricity, heating, cooling, exo- or endothermicity of chemical reactions, etc.)

Operating conditions (temperature, pressure, voltage, current, flow rates, etc.)

Efficiencies

Yield

System capacity (equipment/reactor/plant sizing)

**Key financial parameters** to assess your unit economics, pricing and profitability (some of these might not be assessable until later stages of development, use reference data or proxies until you have your own data):

Capital expenditures (CAPEX): Building, machinery, reactors, equipment, balance of plant, installation, vehicles, IP, etc.

Operational expenditures (OPEX): Electricity, water, steam, chemicals, materials, salaries, rent, maintenance, insurance, etc.

Revenue projections

Profit/profit margins

Payback periods

Net present value (NPV)

Internal rate of return (IRR)

**Sensitivity analyses** to assess the impact of various different commercial and technical parameters. At the pre-seed stage you are likely to be focused on assessing and optimising technical performance parameters, like yield or energy efficiency.

A comparison of your technical and commercial KPIs with those of incumbents and other emerging technologies (**benchmarking**)

**Scaling analysis** (economies of scale + learning effects): Modelling your economics for a first-of-a-kind (FOAK) plant as well as the transition to an Nth-of-a-kind (NOAK) plant

An overview of potential **technical challenges** and **uncertainties** in your model

![](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/39490a_9d5ecee006eb444d88fddc742c83bd22~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_49,h_28,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/39490a_9d5ecee006eb444d88fddc742c83bd22~mv2.png)

*Factors contributing to the operational (OPEX) and capital expenditure (OPEX) of a company and therefore the unit economics.*

Remember: Like a good cuppa tea, your TEA needs to brew. **Don’t expect a perfect outcome (i.e. highly accurate predictions) the first time you build your TEA**. It’s an iterative process that requires time. Your accuracy will improve as you collect more and better data.

Check out the section below for more insights and recommendations on how to build a bullet-proof TEA.

## **What should I keep in mind?**

*Start early*: It is never too early to start building a TEA to identify the levers that are critical for your unit economics.

*Keep it digestible*: A TEA should be well-structured and transparent. Always remember that other people might not be experts in your domain and may therefore require additional guidance. Include an overview page that briefly describes the system, process or product that you are working on and explain your general approach to the TEA.

*Focus on what matters*: Start with the centrepiece of your system/process/product. Follow an 80/20 approach when working on your first versions and do not lose too much time trying to assess non-essential components. Simplify your calculations by making rational assumptions or using trustworthy reference data for supporting components/processes and balance of plant. These can be assessed at a later stage when more accurate data is available to you from testing or partnerships.

*Play with your variables*: Perform a sensitivity analysis (i.e. variation of one or more input variables) to test the assumptions you have made, and how variations affect the overall TEA. Which parameters are the most important?

*Use reliable data*: Any TEA is only as good as the data/assumptions used to build it. Be clear about your assumptions and provide references

*Constantly refine your TEA*: Remember that a TEA is a living document - use it to find your critical path and refine it regularly.

*Benchmark your startup*: Compare your unit economics to those of incumbents and competitors (including pre-commercial, emerging technologies).

*Think ahead*: Identify how you think main variables will change over time (e.g. with regulation, competition, economies of scale, new geographies/markets) and simulate different scenarios.

## **What are common pitfalls?**

At ZCC, we see a huge number of different TEAs every year. We have tried to put together a list of things that can lead to greater levels of uncertainty in your TEA (there is also an excellent Catalyst podcast that talks through some of these in more detail):

*Unreasonable assumptions*:

A very low power price (assuming 100% cheap renewables)

High capacity factors at very low power prices

Low feedstock prices (e.g. for green H2 and biogenic or captured CO2)

Unlimited feedstock availability (e.g. biomass)

Abundant waste heat availability

High green premiums for products with insufficient justification

Aggressive carbon pricing on incumbent technologies to justify own technologies

*Modelling the core technology instead of the full system*: The system boundary should be clearly defined. Take all equipment, engineering and balance of plant into consideration as these can add significant costs.

*Unrealistic or incomplete benchmarking*: It is important to not only benchmark against mature technologies but to also take emerging technologies into account. Compare your costs and sales price to the market prices of these technologies: What is the floor price of incumbents and newcomers? How high can your profit margin be?

*Focusing on the wrong or a limited number of metrics:* A good TEA takes all relevant metrics into consideration.

## Communicating your TEA

The main purpose of building a TEA is to help you understand your business, not simply to fulfil the due diligence requirements of an investor. That said, once built, it can be an extremely powerful tool to communicate with stakeholders: your board, your investors and potential hires. A well structured TEA can show how you think about your technology, your business, and the broader economy in which it sits. How best to communicate your TEA?

**Share the model itself**

investors want to be able to put in their own assumptions

they might be able to help your shape your TEA

**Diagrams!**

Process diagrams

Sensitivity analysis

Waterfall charts to show cost reductions

**Communicate your USP**

where does your technology shine?

## **What are reasonable assumptions for the prices of electricity, hydrogen and carbon dioxide?**

Given that many climate technologies depend heavily on electricity prices, and a significant number, on the cost of hydrogen, carbon dioxide or both, we thought it would be helpful to discuss the numbers here. Please note that all prices mentioned below are bound to change with time.

*Electricity costs:*

Your electricity costs will be heavily dependent on how you choose to source your electricity: will you be grid-connected, use a power purchase agreement (PPA) or be islanded and rely on solar-plus-storage? These are of course different scenarios you can model and determine your strategy accordingly. Where can you find the right values to input into your model?

Prices have shown large variability depending by region, and increased volatility over the last few years. You may need a to build different scenarios for each region, or take this into consideration for planning where to install early deployments.

Prices have shown large variability depending by region, and increased volatility

![](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/39490a_3fad0838b252462fab7abd48a430589b~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_49,h_27,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/39490a_3fad0838b252462fab7abd48a430589b~mv2.png)

*Quarterly average wholesale prices for selected regions in $/MWh (2019-2024). Source:*[*IEA*](https://www.iea.org/reports/electricity-market-report-update-2023/executive-summary)*.*

Our recommendation: €60-80/MWh (~$64-86/MWh) as a starting point

The [Lazard LCOE report](https://www.lazard.com/research-insights/levelized-cost-of-energyplus/) gives values for different technologies (in $)

The lowest LCOE for solar/onshore wind is not available 24/7

Cost of energy available depends on ability to load-follow intermittency

Your technology may be able to load-follow intermittency to take advantage of the cheapest electricity prices, but this will impact capacity factors, and therefore OPEX

Use a sensitivity analysis to evaluate the impact of varying electricity prices

Model different scenarios for different energy procurement strategies

![](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/39490a_f9f0f43a74444e669ebecbb944980bb5~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_49,h_19,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/39490a_f9f0f43a74444e669ebecbb944980bb5~mv2.png)

*Levelised Cost of Energy (LCOE) for different renewable and conventional energy sources. Source:*[*Lazard 2024 LCOE+ Report*](https://www.lazard.com/research-insights/levelized-cost-of-energyplus/)*.*

*Hydrogen costs:*

Electricity costs are the major component of hydrogen costs

Using a €60/MWh electricity cost and assuming energy requirements of 50 kWh/kg H2 (78% efficiency), electrolytic hydrogen production using the same electricity will cost at least €3.0/kg (~$3.2/kg)

There should be a sensible correlation between the electricity price assumed in your TEA and the green hydrogen price

The [Lazard LCOH report](https://www.lazard.com/research-insights/levelized-cost-of-energyplus/) gives estimates of $2.5-6.1/kg for green hydrogen, depending on subsidies

Our recommendation: €3.5/kg (~$3.8/kg H2) as a conservative starting point

Use a sensitivity analysis to capture the uncertainty around future hydrogen prices

![](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/39490a_de8dd9f2b1ce44beb6687b61c2d435be~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_49,h_22,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/39490a_de8dd9f2b1ce44beb6687b61c2d435be~mv2.png)

*Levelised Cost of Hydrogen (LCOH) for green and pink hydrogen. Source:*[*Lazard 2024 LCOE+ Report*](https://www.lazard.com/research-insights/levelized-cost-of-energyplus/)*.*

*Carbon dioxide costs:*

Choosing a carbon dioxide price can be tricky due to the wide range of CDR technologies that are emerging

Costs for point-source captured CO2, biogenic CO2 and some types of biologically captured CO2 fall in the range of approximately €23-93/t CO2 ($25-100/t CO2)

Direct Air Capture (DAC) companies are aiming for €93/t CO2 ($100/t CO2, some even lower) but will inevitably need time to reach these prices

Use a CO2 price that is aligned with your CO2 suppliers/sources (refer to the IEA’s[report](https://www.iea.org/commentaries/is-carbon-capture-too-expensive) on carbon capture costs to get a better understanding for how CO2 costs can vary)

Use a sensitivity analysis to capture the uncertainty around future CO2 prices

![](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/39490a_23414af1d35c4f898f8c5336b17412f0~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_49,h_25,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/39490a_23414af1d35c4f898f8c5336b17412f0~mv2.png)

*Costs of capturing CO2 from different sources in $/t CO2. Source:*[*IEA*](https://www.iea.org/data-and-statistics/charts/levelised-cost-of-co2-capture-by-sector-and-initial-co2-concentration-2019)*.*

## **Where can I learn more about TEAs?**

Need more information or examples? Here is a list of resources that can guide you on your journey to your first TEA:

*What is a TEA?*

[Introduction to TEA](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UHuUtwyLChc) by the U.S. Department of Energy: Full series and PDF versions of training available[here](https://www.energy.gov/eere/iedo/life-cycle-assessment-and-techno-economic-analysis-training).

[Activate’s techno-economics section](https://www.activate.org/techonomics): Features models and videos that can help with early-stage cost modelling. Activate goes back to basics but offers good explanations.

[Catalyst podcast](https://www.canarymedia.com/podcasts/catalyst-with-shayle-kann/climatetech-startups-need-strong-techno-economic-analysis): Discusses TEA pitfalls.

*Templates:*

[Airminer’s templates](https://assessccus.globalco2initiative.org/tea/templates-and-videos/)

[AssessCCUS website and templates](https://assessccus.globalco2initiative.org/tea/templates-and-videos/): Various tips, tricks, links, videos and templates related to TEAs. Here are some of our favourite templates:[Aggregates](https://assessccus.globalco2initiative.org/wp-content/uploads/Aggregates-LCA-and-TEA-Template.xlsx), [Algae](https://assessccus.globalco2initiative.org/wp-content/uploads/Algae-LCA-and-TEA-Template.xlsx), [Chemicals](https://assessccus.globalco2initiative.org/wp-content/uploads/Chemicals-LCA-and-TEA-Template.xlsx), [Concrete](https://assessccus.globalco2initiative.org/wp-content/uploads/Concrete-LCA-and-TEA-Template.xlsx), [Direct Air Capture](https://assessccus.globalco2initiative.org/wp-content/uploads/DAC-LCA-and-TEA-Template.xlsx).

[Planet A’s guide on TEA best practices](https://planet-a.medium.com/a-simple-founders-guide-to-teas-b469f423a2db) - you can access their TEA template[here](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1dRZ8Sjv6lTFQgn7wKETxlFxzp-vixzHFj1xxubJorb4/edit?usp=sharing).

*Data sources & tools:*

[Conductor Labs](https://www.conductor-labs.com/) are building open-access tooling and data repositories for climate founders–watch this space. Learn more about Jesse Lou’s work in this [episode of the Climate Biotech podcast](https://open.spotify.com/episode/0D3M1B9cFb0PDe92ozDdRY).

[AssessCCUS Capital Equipment Cost Database](https://assessccus.globalco2initiative.org/wp-content/uploads/Capital-Equipment-Cost-Database.xlsx): The database contains estimated capital costs (CAPEX) for lots of different equipment. It is centered around CCUS equipment costs but is useful for hard tech in general.

[National Renewable Energy Laboratory (NREL) website](https://www.nrel.gov/analysis/techno-economic.html): Features a Levelised Cost of Energy (LCOE) calculator, manufacturing analysis and solar technology cost analysis tools.

Did we miss something? Are you a founder or investor with an idea that will drastically improve every TEA? Then reach out to us! We would love to learn from you.

# Economic Analysis Tools

The Economic Analysis Tools package provides a means of combining conventional network analyses with economic considerations, to assess the economic impact of network and power plant developments.

The Techno-Economical Calculation (TechEco) function can be used to analyse costs and benefits of network expansion using Net Present Value. TechEco analyses investment costs, cost of losses, interruption costs and the economic impact of project schedules.

Power Park Energy Analysis provides an evaluation of the profitability of power plants based on load flow calculations, including options for time-series and probabilistic analysis.

## Breadcrumb

# Life Cycle Assessment and Techno-Economic Analysis Training

The Industrial Technologies Office (ITO), alongside the Advanced Materials and Manufacturing Technologies Office, has created a repository of resources for assessing emerging technologies on the basis of their potential cost and impact in the commercial marketplace. These resources—including short training videos, tools, and examples—can help users understand impact drivers and quantify the impact potential of a new technology compared to technologies currently available in the marketplace.

**Techno-economic, Energy, & Carbon Heuristic Tool for Early-Stage Technologies (TECHTEST) Tool:** DOE has created TECHTEST to aid users in estimating potential energy, carbon, and cost impacts of a new technology in a streamlined spreadsheet tool that integrates life cycle assessment (LCA) and technoeconomic analysis (TEA) methods. Download the current version of the Excel-based [TECHTEST tool and worked examples](https://www.energy.gov/eere/amo/techno-economic-energy-carbon-heuristic-tool-early-stage-technologies-techtest-tool).

**Tools Library:** DOE's [Strategic Analysis tools](https://www.energy.gov/eere/amo/amo-strategic-analysis-team-manufacturing-materials-and-life-cycle-energy-tools) library includes additional tools that can also support LCA and/or TEA analyses.

**Training Videos:** ITO's series of short tutorial videos explains basic concepts of LCA and TEA in the context of emerging technologies and can support users in assessing impacts for low-TRL technologies and products. See the list below for short descriptions and links to each video (or view the [training videos playlist on YouTube](https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLgU0cHea5t3rTi9c5Rn8UAfR5-225qtbU)).

**Additional Guidance**: ITO's [*Sustainable Materials Selection in Manufactured Products*](https://www.energy.gov/eere/iedo/articles/sustainable-materials-selection-manufactured-products) outlines examples of the types and sources of data needed for energy efficiency-informed decision-making and explores key life cycle considerations for materials selection through a series of practical, cross-sectoral case studies.

## Life Cycle Assessment

[**Introduction to Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)**](#LCA): LCA is a methodology for assessing the impacts associated with the entire life cycle of a product or process.

[**Estimating Raw Material Embodied Energy Use**](#rawmaterial): Raw Materials Extraction and Processing is the first phase in the product life cycle, and energy associated with raw input materials are "embodied" into manufactured products.

[**Estimating Manufacturing Energy Consumption**](#ManufacturingEnergyConsumption): Manufacturing is the second phase in a product’s life cycle. This life cycle phase can include impacts associated with onsite fuel combustion at manufacturing sites, offsite electricity generation, and non-energy-related process emissions.

[**Estimating Use Phase Energy**](#UsePhaseEnergy): In life cycle assessment, the use phase of a manufactured product captures the impacts of product use. When a new technology replaces an existing product, this can result in a shift in energy consumption during the product’s use or disposal.

## Techno-Economic Analysis

[**Introduction to Techno-Economic Analysis (TEA)**](#TEA): TEA is a method for evaluating the economic performance of a technology.

[**Estimating Manufacturing Costs for Pre-Commercial Technologies**](#emc): Manufacturing costs can be broken down into two main categories: capital expenses and operating expenses.

## Analytical Basics

[**Defining Functional Units for LCA and TEA**](#functionalunit): The functional unit describes a quantity of product or product system on the basis of the performance it delivers in its end-use application.

[**Technology Benchmarking for Comparative LCA and TEA**](#techbenchmarking): The commercial benchmark technology is the primary technology in the marketplace that the new technology would displace.

#### TECHTEST Tool

[**TECHTEST Tool Tutorial**](#TECHTESTToolTutorial): Estimate potential energy and cost impacts of a new technology using DOE's Techno-economic, Energy, and Carbon Heuristic Tool for Early Stage Technologies ([TECHTEST](https://www.energy.gov/eere/iedo/techno-economic-energy-carbon-heuristic-tool-early-stage-technologies-techtest-tool)), a streamlined spreadsheet tool that integrates simplified life cycle assessment (LCA) and technoeconomic analysis (TEA) methods.

View the Training Videos:

Life cycle assessment (LCA) is a methodology for assessing the impacts associated with the entire life cycle of a particular product or process. This video introduces LCA methodologies and explains how LCA techniques can be used to assess the impacts of a manufactured product holistically. Three different LCA approaches are introduced: attributional LCA, consequential LCA, and a hybrid (benchmarking) LCA approach.

Download a [**static PDF**](https://www.energy.gov/sites/default/files/2022-07/2022-07-14%20-%20Intro%20to%20LCA%20-%20Slides%20and%20Transcript_compliant_0.pdf) version of this tutorial video that includes key schematic illustrations from the video and the corresponding transcript.

[Back to top](#top)

**Introduction to Techno-Economic Analysis (TEA)**

Techno-Economic Analysis (TEA) is a method for evaluating the economic performance of a technology. This video introduces TEA analysis concepts and estimation methods, describing a cost benchmarking approach that focuses on comparing an emerging technology to an existing commercial benchmark.

Download a static [**PDF version**](https://www.energy.gov/sites/default/files/2022-01/2022-01-19%20-%20Intro%20to%20TEA%20-%20Slides%20and%20Transcript_compliant_1_0.pdf) of this tutorial video that includes key schematic illustrations from the video and the corresponding transcript.

[Back to top](#top)

**Defining Functional Units for LCA and TEA**

The functional unit describes a quantity of product or product system on the basis of the performance it delivers in its end-use application. This video describes the purpose of the functional unit in LCA and TEA analyses, provides examples of functional units for different products and technologies, and discusses considerations for selecting the best functional unit.

Download a static [PDF version](https://www.energy.gov/sites/default/files/2022-06/2022-05-03%20-%20Functional%20Unit%20PDF_compliant.pdf) of this tutorial video that includes key schematic illustrations from the video and the corresponding transcript.

[Back to top](#top)

**Technology Benchmarking for Comparative LCA and TEA**

The commercial benchmark technology is the primary technology in the marketplace that the new technology would displace. This video discusses best practices for identifying commercial benchmarks for use in LCA or TEA analyses.

Download a static [PDF version](https://www.energy.gov/sites/default/files/2022-06/2022-05-03%20-%20Commercial%20Benchmark%20PDF_compliant.pdf) of this tutorial video that includes key schematic illustrations from the video and the corresponding transcript.

[Back to top](#top)

**Estimating Manufacturing Cost**

Manufacturing costs can be broken down into two main categories: capital expenses (one-time facility costs such as equipment, buildings and construction) and operating expenses (recurring costs such as materials, labor, and energy). This video describes techniques for estimating manufacturing costs in each of these categories, focusing on methods that can be used to support techno-economic analysis of early-stage technologies. Techniques are demonstrated through an example.

Download a static [PDF version](https://www.energy.gov/sites/default/files/2023-01/2022-11-29%20-%20Mfg%20Costs%20PDF%20%281%29_0.pdf) of this tutorial video that includes key schematic illustrations from the video and the corresponding transcript. Access the [Excel-based Labor Estimator Mini-Tool](https://www.energy.gov/eere/iedo/labor-cost-estimator-mini-tool) as an example of a “rule of thumb” approach for estimating labor requirements based on process type and complexity.

[Back to top](#top)

**Estimating Raw Material Embodied Energy**

Embodied energy (or cumulative energy demand) is the sum of all energy inputs required to create a product. This video focuses on estimating these quantities for the first phase in the product life cycle: raw materials extraction and processing.

Download a static [PDF version](https://www.energy.gov/sites/default/files/2023-01/2022-12-14%20-%20Raw%20Materials%20PDF%20%281%29.pdf) of this tutorial video that includes key schematic illustrations from the video and the corresponding transcript. Access the free [Materials Flows through Industry (MFI) tool](https://mfitool.nrel.gov/) and a simple [Excel-based mini-tool](/eere/ammto/material-flow-through-industry-data-extractor-tool "Material Flow Through Industry Data Extractor Tool") to extract embodied energy from an MFI data output file.

[Back to top](#top)

**Estimating Manufacturing Energy Consumption**

Manufacturing is the second phase in a product's life cycle. This life cycle phase can include impacts associated with onsite fuel combustion at manufacturing sites, offsite electricity generation, and non-energy-related process emissions. Estimating manufacturing energy consumption is the second phase in the product life cycle. This video reviews the types of energy consumed by manufacturing facilities and describes techniques for estimating manufacturing energy based on facility or process information.

Download a static [PDF version](https://cms.doe.gov/sites/default/files/2023-07/2023-04-14%20-%20Manufacturing%20Energy%20and%20Emissions%20PDF_0.pdf) of this tutorial video that includes key schematic illustrations from the video and the corresponding transcript.

[Back to top](#top)

**Estimating Use Phase Energy**

In life cycle assessment, the use phase of a manufactured product captures the impacts of product use. When a new technology replaces an existing product, this can result in a shift in energy consumption during the product's use or disposal. This video describes techniques for estimating the energy associated with the use phase of a manufactured product. The video also describes analytical boundaries for a use phase assessment and methods for scaling use phase impacts for an individual product to overall impacts for the United States.

Download a static [PDF version](https://cms.doe.gov/sites/default/files/2023-07/2023-04-14%20-%20Use%20Phase%20PDF.pdf) of this tutorial video that includes key schematic illustrations from the video and the corresponding transcript.

[Back to top](#top)

**Techno-economic Energy Heuristic Tool for Early-Stage Technologies (TECHTEST) Tool Tutorial**

Estimate potential energy and cost impacts of a new technology using DOE’s Techno-economic Energy Heuristic Tool for Early Stage Technologies ([TECHTEST](https://www.energy.gov/eere/iedo/techno-economic-energy-carbon-heuristic-tool-early-stage-technologies-techtest-tool)), a streamlined spreadsheet tool that integrates simplified life cycle assessment (LCA) and technoeconomic analysis (TEA) methods. This video offers an overview of the TECHTEST tool and demonstrates the tool’s features by working through an example.

Access the current version of [DOE’s TECHTEST tool](https://www.energy.gov/eere/iedo/techno-economic-energy-carbon-heuristic-tool-early-stage-technologies-techtest-tool). Download a static [PDF version](https://cms.doe.gov/sites/default/files/2023-07/2023-04-14%20-%20TECHTEST%20PDF.pdf) of this tutorial video that includes key schematic illustrations from the video and the corresponding transcript.

[Back to top](#top)

enriched_points.expanded_content: Techno-Economic Analysis (TEA) rigorously assesses both capital and operational costs, ensuring technologies are economically viable and competitive within their respective markets.
TEA emphasizes understanding Capital Expenditures (CapEx) and Operating Expenses (OpEx), allowing stakeholders to evaluate initial investments and ongoing operational costs effectively.
Advanced tools, like NREL's LCOE calculator and DOE's TECHTEST, enhance TEA precision by integrating data analytics for detailed cost estimations and performance assessments.
TEA is applied in the energy sector to optimize technology deployment, assess renewable energy projects, and ensure compliance with economic and environmental goals.
